Q-values converge in a single step since zero discounting simplifies the Bellman equation to V(s) = r_s
jsonData.explanation: With γ = 0, the discounted return G = r_1 + 0·r_2 + 0²·r_3 + … = r_1. The agent cares only about the immediate reward, making it myopic. It will take the action with the highest immediate reward even if it leads to very poor long-term outcomes. Option D is partially true (Bellman simplifies) but convergence in 'one step' is incorrect — the agent still needs many episodes.

jsonData.question: A practitioner fits PCA on the entire dataset (train + test combined), then splits it for model evaluation. What problem does this introduce?
options: Underfitting — PCA discards variance components that the model relies on to distinguish the classes
Label leakage — PCA incorporates class labels from the test set into the principal component directions
Computational inefficiency — fitting PCA on the full dataset is more expensive than on training data alone
Data leakage — test set statistics contaminate the PCA transformation applied to training data, inflating evaluation scores
jsonData.explanation: PCA computes eigenvectors from the covariance matrix of whatever data it sees. Fitting on train + test means test-set variance influences the transformation used to preprocess training data. The model is indirectly 'seeing' test distribution information during training, producing optimistically biased evaluation results. PCA is unsupervised so it uses no labels (option B is wrong).

jsonData.question: What are the two main disadvantages of the simple train-test split approach?
options: Data may be non-representative; only part of available data is tested on
It always causes overfitting and wastes too much computational training time
Test data is never used for training; data splits cannot be randomized at all
It requires labeled validation data and always causes underfitting issues here
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly lists two problems: (1) a random split may produce a non-representative sample, and (2) the model is tested on only a fraction of available data. Cross-Validation solves both. Option B is incorrect — train-test split does not always cause overfitting.

jsonData.question: In K-fold cross-validation, how is the final error estimate E computed from the K fold errors?
options: E = max(E_1, E_2, ..., E_K) — worst-case fold error across all folds
E = (1/K) × Σ E_i — the arithmetic mean of all K individual fold errors
E = min(E_1, E_2, ..., E_K) — the best achieved fold error across folds
E = the median of all K fold errors after sorting them in ascending order
jsonData.explanation: The lecture shows the formula E = (1/K) Σ_{i=1}^{K} E_i — the average over all K fold errors. Using max or min would be biased; median is not the standard. Taking the mean ensures all parts of the data equally contribute to the final estimate.

jsonData.question: In Algorithm 8 (CrossValidate), what does the variable â store at the end of execution?
options: The number of folds K used in the cross-validation procedure
The minimum cross-validation error achieved across all hyperparameter settings
The hyperparameter setting that yielded the lowest cross-validation error
The average of all error estimates computed during the K-fold procedure
jsonData.explanation: The algorithm comment states: â stores 'the hyperparameter setting that yielded it (the lowest error).' Option B describes ê (the stored best error value), not â (the hyperparameter setting). These two are distinct variables in the algorithm.

jsonData.question: What is the primary benefit of Leave-One-Out cross-validation according to the lecture?
options: It is computationally cheap because it trains far fewer models than K-fold
It always outperforms K-fold CV on large datasets with well-balanced classes
It reduces variance by using training sets that are highly dissimilar from each other
It uses data very efficiently and gives a nearly unbiased performance estimate
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states LOO provides 'very efficient use of limited data' and 'gives an almost unbiased estimate of model performance.' Option A is wrong — LOO is actually computationally expensive (trains N models). Option C is wrong — LOO training sets are nearly identical, which causes high variance, not low variance.

jsonData.question: What is Leave-One-Out (LOO) cross-validation defined as?
options: K-fold cross-validation where K equals the total number of data points N
A method that randomly leaves out 10% of the data for testing each time
K-fold CV where a single class label is excluded from training to test generalization
Cross-validation that reserves one hyperparameter setting as the held-out test condition
jsonData.explanation: LOO is defined as K-fold CV with K=N, meaning each single data point serves as the test set exactly once, with all remaining N-1 points used for training. This is why it gives an unbiased estimate — every sample participates in evaluation.

jsonData.question: A model achieves near-perfect training accuracy but very poor test accuracy. According to the debugging strategies, what is the likely cause?
options: The representation is inadequate and requires better input feature engineering
The problem is in generalization — the model family may be too complex
The implementation contains a bug that is artificially inflating training accuracy
The training and test data distributions are perfectly matched and balanced here
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's strategy: if the learner CAN fit training data but fails on test data, the problem is in generalization. A too-complex model family is one cause (overfitting). Option A (representation) applies when the model CANNOT fit training data — the opposite scenario.

jsonData.question: Which of the following correctly states BOTH drawbacks of LOO cross-validation?
options: Low bias and low variance, making it too conservative for most real datasets
Requires too much labeled data and always causes complete overfitting of models
Computationally expensive (must train N models) and high variance in error estimates
Produces heavily biased estimates because each test set contains only a single sample
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly lists both: (1) Computationally expensive — must train N models; (2) High variance — training sets are extremely similar, leading to inconsistent error estimates. Option D confuses bias (LOO is nearly unbiased) with variance. Option A contradicts the lecture's stated drawback.

jsonData.question: To verify that a learning algorithm's implementation is correct, what does the lecture recommend measuring instead of accuracy?
options: Precision, recall, and F1 on the validation set across each training epoch run
Gradient norm to confirm the optimizer is converging toward a local minimum
AUC-ROC on a held-out test set to confirm the ranking output is correct
Loss (to confirm it is being minimized) rather than classification accuracy
jsonData.explanation: The lecture says: 'Measure loss rather than accuracy (make sure it is minimized)' to check implementation correctness. A bug might still show reasonable accuracy but will be revealed if the loss is not decreasing. The other metrics (F1, gradient norm, AUC-ROC) are not mentioned for this specific debugging purpose.

jsonData.question: After adding a cheating feature that perfectly correlates with the correct class, the model achieves near 0% training error. What does this indicate according to the lecture?
options: The representation is potentially adequate; focus on better features or model change
There is definitely a critical implementation bug in the learning algorithm code
The model is severely overfitting and the cheating feature must be removed immediately
The training data is too small and must be substantially augmented with new examples
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'If (near) 0% error: work on better features, or change learning model.' Near-0% error with the cheating feature means the algorithm CAN overfit when given perfect information — so the issue is the features, not a bug. Option B (bug) applies when even the cheating feature fails to push error near 0%.

jsonData.question: When you suspect a train/test distribution mismatch is causing poor performance, what strategy does the lecture recommend?
options: Re-collect an entirely new dataset that better reflects the actual test distribution
Reshuffle the training and test data together, then re-split them randomly
Apply domain adaptation techniques to align the training data distribution better
Increase training data proportion using a 90-10 train/test split instead of 80-20
jsonData.explanation: The lecture prescribes: 'Try reselecting train/test by shuffling training and test data together.' This checks if the mismatch was an artifact of how data was split. Domain adaptation (option C) is a solution technique, not a diagnostic strategy as described in the lecture.

jsonData.question: According to the debugging strategies, if a learner CANNOT fit the training data at all, what type of problem does this indicate?
options: An overfitting problem requiring a simpler model family with fewer parameters
A generalization problem requiring more data to reduce variance in the model
A representation problem requiring better features or higher-quality data
A hyperparameter problem requiring more exhaustive cross-validation grid search
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's diagnosis tree: if the model CANNOT fit training data → the problem is in representation (need better features or better data). If it CAN fit training but not test → generalization problem. Option A (overfitting) is wrong because a model that can't even fit training data is underfitting, not overfitting.

jsonData.question: In Algorithm 8 (CrossValidate), what initial value is assigned to ê, and why?
options: 0 — so any positive error encountered will immediately update the stored best value
1.0 — representing 100% error as the worst possible classification performance
The average error from the first complete K-fold run on the full training dataset
∞ (infinity) — ensuring any finite real error computed will immediately update it
jsonData.explanation: The algorithm initializes ê ← ∞ so that the first hyperparameter setting evaluated will always produce avgErr < ê and become the initial best. If initialized to 0, no setting could improve it. The algorithm comment says 'store lowest error encountered so far.'

jsonData.question: How does the lecture suggest testing whether you have enough training data for a model?
options: Train on progressively smaller subsets and measure how much performance drops
Compare training accuracy directly against test accuracy on the full available dataset
Apply LOO cross-validation and compare its variance to standard K-fold CV results
Measure the ratio of training examples to model parameters as a complexity proxy
jsonData.explanation: The lecture prescribes: 'Try training on less training set, how much does it hurt performance?' If performance degrades sharply with less data, you likely need more. This learning-curve-style analysis directly measures data dependency. Options B, C, and D are not mentioned in this context.

jsonData.question: In the CrossValidate algorithm, how is the test set for iteration k formally defined?
options: All examples where n mod K equals 0, forming a fixed reserved holdout set
All examples where n mod K = k−1, selecting every K-th example starting at k
Examples randomly sampled without replacement from the complete dataset
All examples excluded from the training set used in the previous iteration's fold
jsonData.explanation: The algorithm defines: test ← {(x_n, y_n) ∈ Data : n mod K = k−1}. This partitions the dataset into K equally-sized non-overlapping folds by index. Option D sounds plausible but the folds are pre-defined by the modulo formula, not derived from the previous iteration's exclusions.

jsonData.question: Why does LOO cross-validation exhibit high variance in its error estimates?
options: Each single test example creates highly noisy estimates from just one data point per fold
The model must train on only N-1 examples, which is insufficient for algorithm convergence
The N training sets are nearly identical, producing highly correlated and inconsistent estimates
Each individual test fold contains only a single example, making single-fold errors extremely unreliable and noisy in practice
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'High variance — training sets are extremely similar, so results not similar.' Because each of N training sets differs by only one data point, the learned models are nearly identical (highly correlated). Correlated estimates do not average out well, increasing variance in the final estimate. Option A is tempting but the lecture attributes the cause to training set similarity.

jsonData.question: In the K=10 fold cross-validation example shown in the lecture, what does the final error E equal?
options: E = E_1 × E_2 × ... × E_10 (product of all ten individual fold errors)
E = (E_max − E_min) representing the performance range across all ten folds
E = (E_1 + E_10) / 2, averaging only the best and worst fold performance scores
E = (1/10) × Σ_{i=1}^{10} E_i, the mean of all ten individual fold errors
jsonData.explanation: The formula shown in the lecture diagram is E = (1/10) Σ_{i=1}^{10} E_i — the arithmetic mean of all 10 fold errors. This generalizes to (1/K) Σ E_i for any K. Using products or only two points would distort the estimate.

jsonData.question: What is the purpose of hand-crafting a toy dataset when debugging a learning algorithm?
options: To verify that the implementation produces correct outputs on known, controlled inputs
To check whether the model architecture is too complex for the given learning task
To measure how much the model's test performance degrades when training data is reduced
To simulate real-world deployment conditions and verify the model generalizes broadly enough
jsonData.explanation: The lecture lists 'hand-craft a toy dataset' as one strategy to check 'Is learning algorithm implementation correct?' A toy dataset with known correct outputs lets you verify whether the algorithm produces the expected results, exposing bugs. Options C and D describe different debugging strategies also in the lecture.

jsonData.question: According to the debugging strategies, when a learner fits training data well but fails on test data, what does a 'too complicated model family' indicate?
options: An underfitting problem requiring additional features to boost model expressiveness
An overfitting problem where the model has memorized rather than learned patterns
A data noise problem requiring cleaning or denoising the training samples first
A representation problem requiring fundamentally different model architectures overall
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: if the learner CAN fit training data but fails on test data and the cause is 'too complicated model family' → this is overfitting. The model memorizes training data and fails to generalize. Underfitting (option A) is the opposite — when the model cannot even fit training data.

jsonData.question: When the learner CAN fit training data but struggles on test data, the lecture identifies two possible generalization failure causes. What are they?
options: Noisy training data and incorrect feature scaling across the two data splits
Buggy implementation and a train/test distribution mismatch in the dataset
Too complicated model family (overfitting) or not enough data for the problem
Insufficient regularization and a poorly chosen evaluation metric for the task
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly lists: (1) too complicated model family → overfit; (2) not enough data → underfit. Both appear under the 'Yes: problem is in generalization to test data' branch of the debugging decision tree. Options A and D are not mentioned in this context.

jsonData.question: When a learner cannot fit the training data, what solution does the debugging strategy in the lecture prescribe?
options: Increase model capacity by adding more hidden layers or learnable parameters
Apply cross-validation to find better hyperparameters for the existing model
Collect substantially more training data to reduce algorithm variance overall
Improve the feature representation or collect higher-quality training data
jsonData.explanation: When the model cannot fit training data, the problem is representation. The lecture prescribes: 'need better features' or 'better data.' Option A (more capacity) might help but is not what the lecture recommends for this specific case. Option C (more data) is the remedy for a different problem — data sufficiency.

jsonData.question: According to the lecture, LOO cross-validation is described as especially natural for which type of learning algorithm?
options: k-Nearest Neighbor (kNN) algorithms
Support Vector Machines (SVM) with kernel functions
Deep neural networks with gradient-based optimization procedures
Decision trees using recursive binary splitting and pruning strategies
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly states: 'Very natural for kNN.' This is because kNN doesn't require explicit retraining — the 'model' is just the stored training data, so leaving one point out and predicting it is computationally trivial. The other algorithms (SVM, deep nets, decision trees) require non-trivial retraining for each of the N folds.

jsonData.question: After adding a cheating feature, a model STILL fails to achieve near 0% training error. According to the lecture, what does this indicate?
options: The model generalizes well and the cheating feature is completely unnecessary
Too many noisy features exist, or there is a bug in the implementation
The model has converged optimally and additional training epochs are not needed
Cross-validation error is likely lower than the reported held-out test error shows
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'If not (near) 0% error: too many noisy features OR a bug!' If even a perfectly correlated cheating feature cannot drive training error near zero, the model is either being confused by noise in other features or contains a bug preventing it from learning. Option A is wrong — the model is clearly not fitting the training data.

jsonData.question: Without inductive bias, what does the lecture say would happen to a machine learning model?
options: It would always select the most complex hypothesis available in the hypothesis space
It would memorize all training examples verbatim, achieving 100% training accuracy
It would always produce random predictions regardless of patterns in the training data
It would be unable to generalize from training data to new unseen problems
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly states: 'Without inductive bias, the model cannot generalize from training data to new unseen problems.' Inductive bias is not optional — without it the model has no basis for choosing among hypotheses that equally fit the training data. Options A and B describe overfitting behavior, not the consequence of having no inductive bias.

jsonData.question: Which of the following is NOT listed in the lecture as a reason ML might fail on a task?
options: Noisy training data where the corruption can appear in either features or labels
Using a model architecture that is too simple for the task complexity
Features that are insufficient, irrelevant, or provide no useful signal
Mismatch between the learner's inductive bias and the target concept being learned
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's failure list includes: very small training data, noisy data (in features or labels), unhelpful/insufficient features, examples without a single correct answer, and inductive bias mismatch. 'Model architecture too simple' is not explicitly listed — it is a consequence of underfitting but not itemized as a distinct failure reason. Options A, C, and D appear directly on the lecture's failure list.

jsonData.question: In the animal-labeling example where 60–70% of people chose the bird/non-bird (ABBA) grouping, the lecture uses this scenario to illustrate:
options: That different learners apply different inductive biases from the same training data
That majority-vote labeling always determines the correct classification boundary
That humans consistently outperform machines when the training data is ambiguous
That the total number of training examples determines which labeling scheme is correct
jsonData.explanation: The lecture concludes: 'It is a bias that different human learners have. But in machine is called Inductive bias.' Neither the ABBA nor AABB labeling is objectively correct from the data alone — both are consistent with the training examples. The split (60–70% vs 30–40%) reflects different underlying assumptions (bird/non-bird vs can-fly/cannot-fly), which is exactly the definition of inductive bias.

jsonData.question: In the lecture's 3-points-on-paper example, a learner prefers drawing a straight line over a curve through the same points. This preference represents:
options: Underfitting, since a straight line has insufficient capacity to fit all 3 training points
Inductive bias — the model's prior preference for simpler solutions over complex ones
Regularization, which imposes an explicit mathematical penalty on models deviating from linearity
Overfitting, since both the straight line and the curve explain the same training points equally
jsonData.explanation: The lecture concludes this example with: 'inductive bias = the model's preference or assumption that helps it choose one solution among many possible ones.' Preferring the simplest solution (straight line) when both fit equally well is the textbook definition of inductive bias. Option A is wrong because both options fit the 3 training points; option C describes a mechanism that can encode bias but is not the bias concept itself.

jsonData.question: A classification algorithm predicts a test point's label by finding its 3 closest training examples and taking a majority vote. This algorithm's inductive bias is best described as:
options: Linearity — the model fits a weighted combination of features to a straight decision boundary
Locality — nearby points in feature space are assumed to belong to the same class
Parsimony — models using fewer training neighbors are preferred as simpler alternatives
Conditional Independence — features are unrelated to each other given the class label
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's inductive bias table assigns KNN the 'Locality' bias: 'Nearby points belong to the same class (Similar things are close to each other).' Using 3 nearest neighbors directly embodies this assumption. Parsimony belongs to Decision Trees, Linearity to Linear Regression, and Conditional Independence to Naive Bayes — none of which applies to KNN.

jsonData.question: A spam classifier treats the presence of 'free' in an email as completely independent of 'money' appearing, given the spam/not-spam label. This reflects which inductive bias?
options: Locality — each email's classification depends on the messages most similar to it in feature space
Hierarchy — spam features can be organized in a tree structure of ranked decision conditions
Linearity — spam probability is a weighted linear combination of all individual word features
Conditional Independence — all features are independent given the class, as in Naive Bayes
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's table assigns Naive Bayes the 'Conditional Independence' bias: 'Every feature is completely independent of any other feature given the class label.' Treating each word independently given the spam label is the defining Naive Bayes assumption. Locality belongs to KNN, Hierarchy/Parsimony to Decision Trees, and Linearity to Linear Regression.

jsonData.question: A practitioner adjusts the maximum depth of a Decision Tree to balance overfitting and underfitting. Based on the lecture, max_depth is classified as a:
options: Model parameter, since DecisionTreeTrain learns the best depth value directly from training data
Loss function setting, since it directly determines the training error computed at each split node
Regularization term that explicitly penalizes the model for every additional tree split added
Hyperparameter, since it controls other model parameters and tunes the inductive bias
jsonData.explanation: The lecture defines hyperparameters as 'parameters that control other model parameters of the model.' Max depth controls how many nodes and branches (the true parameters) the tree can develop. The lecture explicitly uses DT depth as the canonical hyperparameter example, contrasting it with tree nodes/branches, which are learned parameters. Regularization terms are a separate concept not used to define depth here.

jsonData.question: A researcher evaluates five DT depth settings by testing each model's accuracy on the test set, then selects the best depth and reports that test accuracy as the final result. What is wrong with this approach?
options: The test set is generally too small to give statistically reliable accuracy comparisons
Accuracy is not informative when the class distribution in the test set is heavily imbalanced
Hyperparameters must always be tuned exclusively on held-out data, never on the test set, or data contamination occurs and the results become unreliable
Tuning on the test set contaminates it, making the final error estimate optimistically biased
jsonData.explanation: The lecture warns explicitly: 'Never ever touch your test data!' Using the test set for hyperparameter selection means the reported test accuracy no longer measures true generalization — it is biased toward the selected model, just as training accuracy is biased toward the training data. Hyperparameter tuning must only use the development (validation) set. Options A and B describe different problems unrelated to this violation.

jsonData.question: A hypothesis h is formally said to overfit the training data when which condition holds?
options: error_true(h) strictly exceeds error_train(h), the formal definition of overfitting
error_train(h) equals zero while the model still produces non-zero error on all unseen examples
The model complexity significantly exceeds the total number of available training examples
Both the training loss and the test loss converge to the same non-zero finite value
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'We say h overfits the training data if error_true(h) > error_train(h).' This is the precise formal condition. Option B describes an extreme special case (zero training error) but overfitting is defined whenever true error exceeds training error, not just when training error hits zero. Option C is a common intuition but not the formal definition given.

jsonData.question: Which statement correctly describes underfitting according to the lecture?
options: The model has too many parameters relative to the number of available training examples
The algorithm had the opportunity to learn more from training data, but failed to do so
The model memorizes specific training examples rather than learning general patterns
The inductive bias of the model perfectly aligns with the target concept, preventing any errors
jsonData.explanation: The lecture defines underfitting as: 'Learning algorithm had the opportunity to learn more from training data, but didn't. Or didn't have sufficient data to learn from.' Option A describes a cause of overfitting (too many parameters), and option C describes overfitting behavior (memorizing training examples). Underfitting means the model is under-capacitated, not the inductive bias is perfect.

jsonData.question: What is the key distinction between overfitting and underfitting as described in the lecture?
options: Overfitting: error_true > error_train (model fits noise); Underfitting: model failed to fully learn from training data
Overfitting simultaneously raises both training and test accuracy, while underfitting only reduces training accuracy
Underfitting occurs when the model uses too many parameters relative to data; overfitting with too few
Both phenomena stem from the same root cause — data noise — but manifest on different evaluation subsets
jsonData.explanation: The lecture formally defines overfitting as error_true(h) > error_train(h) — the model learns noise specific to training data. Underfitting occurs when 'the algorithm had the opportunity to learn more from training data, but didn't.' Option B is wrong: overfitting specifically causes test accuracy to decrease while training accuracy keeps rising, as shown in the lecture's accuracy curve. They have distinct causes and distinct fixes.

jsonData.question: According to the lecture, what is the development set specifically used for?
options: Learning model parameters such as the nodes, branches, and thresholds of a Decision Tree
Estimating the model's final performance on completely new examples not seen during training
Tuning hyperparameters such as the maximum depth setting of a Decision Tree
Providing additional training data when the main training set is too small to learn from
jsonData.explanation: The lecture specifies: 'Development set — used to learn/tune hyper-parameters, e.g., max depth of decision tree.' Option A describes the training set's role (learning nodes/branches, which are parameters). Option B describes the test set's role (evaluating unseen performance). The dev set sits between training and test, serving exclusively for hyperparameter selection.

jsonData.question: When selecting the optimal depth for a Decision Tree by comparing multiple depth settings, which data split should be used to make that selection?
options: Training set, since all learning decisions including hyperparameter choices are ultimately data-driven
Test set, since its unbiased estimates make it more accurate for hyperparameter comparison decisions
Development (validation) set, which is held out specifically for hyperparameter tuning
Any of the three sets, since hyperparameter selection is statistically independent of which subset is used
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's general approach states: 'For each possible setting of your hyper-parameters: Train a model on the training data. Compute this model's error rate on the development data.' The dev set is specifically designed for this purpose. The test set must never be used for selection, as doing so would contaminate it and invalidate the final evaluation.

jsonData.question: According to the lecture's recommended general approach, what is the correct data split ratio?
options: 60% training, 20% development, and 20% testing
80% training, 10% development, and 10% testing
70% training, 10% development, and 20% testing
50% training, 25% development, and 25% testing
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly states: 'Split your data into 70% training data, 10% development data and 20% test data.' This allocation gives the majority to training (for learning), a small portion to development (for hyperparameter tuning), and a held-out 20% for final test evaluation. Options A and D give too much data to non-training sets.

jsonData.question: What does the lecture say about a Decision Tree trained with max_depth = 0 versus max_depth = ∞?
options: depth = 0 produces the empty tree (underfitting); depth = ∞ produces the full tree (overfitting)
depth = 0 produces the full tree (overfitting); depth = ∞ produces the empty tree (underfitting)
Both settings produce high training accuracy, but the full tree achieves better test performance
Both settings generalize equally well, since tree depth does not control the overfitting behavior
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'By playing with this depth knob, we can adjust between underfitting (the empty tree, depth = 0) and overfitting (the full tree, depth = ∞).' Option B reverses this relationship entirely. The full tree memorizes every training example (0% training error) but fails to generalize — the classic definition of overfitting.

jsonData.question: A fraud detection system organizes customer features hierarchically to make decisions, preferring compact rule trees over deep complex ones. This reflects which inductive bias?
options: Locality — fraud patterns among nearest neighbors in feature space are assumed to cluster together
Hierarchy and Parsimony — features fit a tree structure; simpler trees are preferred
Sphericality — each fraud cluster is assumed to be round with one central representative point
Conditional Independence — each feature independently signals fraud regardless of other features
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's table assigns Decision Trees the 'Hierarchy/Parsimony' bias: 'Features can be organized in a tree structure; shorter/simpler trees are preferred.' This scenario — hierarchical rules with a preference for simplicity — directly maps to this bias. Locality (KNN), Sphericality (K-Means), and Conditional Independence (Naive Bayes) each belong to different algorithms.

jsonData.question: In the proper evaluation formalization from the lecture, the expected error formula ε = E_{(x,y)~D}[ℓ(y, f(x))] represents:
options: The expected error of function f over the true, unknown data distribution D
The average training loss computed over the N labeled examples in the training set
The maximum loss achievable by any hypothesis selected from the hypothesis space
The empirical error measured over a fixed held-out test set after training is complete
jsonData.explanation: The lecture defines ε as the expectation of the loss ℓ(y, f(x)) taken over (x, y) drawn from the true distribution D — the unknown distribution we are trying to generalize to. Option B describes error_train, which is an empirical average over the training set only. The key distinction is that ε integrates over the full (unknown) distribution, not observed samples.

jsonData.question: According to the inductive bias table in the lecture, K-Means clustering operates under which core assumption?
options: Features can be organized hierarchically; simpler trees with fewer levels are strongly preferred
All features are conditionally independent of each other given the assigned cluster label
Clusters are spherical in shape and each cluster is well-described by one central centroid
Similar points share labels; the decision boundary follows the nearest-neighbor Voronoi rule
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's table assigns K-Means the 'Sphericality/Centroids' assumption: 'Clusters are spherical (round) and each cluster can be represented by a single central point.' This is why K-Means fails on elongated or irregularly shaped clusters. Option A (hierarchy) belongs to Decision Trees; option B (conditional independence) to Naive Bayes; option D (locality/nearest-neighbor) to KNN.

jsonData.question: A linear regression model trained on millions of clean, well-labeled cat images still fails at cat detection. The most applicable failure cause from the lecture's failure list is:
options: Noisy training labels sending inconsistent supervision signals throughout the dataset
Insufficient training data preventing the model from learning the visual classification signal
Mismatch between the linearity inductive bias and the inherently non-linear visual concept
Overfitting on training examples because a linear model has too many adjustable parameters
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly lists 'Mismatch between inductive bias of learner and concept we aim to learn' as a failure reason. Linear regression assumes the output is a weighted linear combination of inputs — a strong linearity bias. Visual concepts like 'is this a cat?' are highly non-linear and cannot be captured by a linear model regardless of data quantity. The abundance of clean, labeled data rules out options A and B directly.

jsonData.question: What does the term 'hypothesis space' represent in supervised machine learning?
options: The collection of all possible training datasets the algorithm could theoretically encounter
The range of accuracy values achievable given a fixed dataset and a fixed learning algorithm
The set of all input-output pairs that define the unknown true data distribution
The set of all functions or models the learning algorithm can potentially choose from
jsonData.explanation: The lecture shows the hypothesis space H as the source from which the learning algorithm selects a final hypothesis. It contains all possible mappings from inputs to outputs that the model class can represent. Option A describes possible training sets; option C describes the data distribution. The learning algorithm searches the hypothesis space to find the function that minimizes error on training data.

jsonData.question: In a Decision Tree, what is the role of terminal (leaf) nodes?
options: They output the final decision: a class label or regression score
They perform feature value queries to determine which branch to follow at each step
They store the training examples assigned to each subtree during learning
They define the splitting criterion evaluated at each level of the tree
jsonData.explanation: Terminal (leaf) nodes are the decision nodes — they output the final result: a class label for classification or an output score for regression. Internal nodes perform the feature queries (e.g., 'Is x_i > 0.5?'). Terminal nodes are reached after traversing all the splits.

jsonData.question: What does the acronym 'Bagging' stand for in ensemble learning?
options: Bootstrap Aggregating and Growing of decision trees
Bootstrap Aggregating
Bayesian Aggregating and Generating of weak models
Batch Aggregating and Grouping of ensemble models
jsonData.explanation: Bagging stands for 'Bootstrap Aggregating' — it consists of two principles: Bootstrap Sampling (training on random subsets) and Aggregating (combining results via majority vote or averaging). Options A, C, and D are fabricated expansions of the acronym.

jsonData.question: Who proposed the Bagging method, and in what year?
options: Geoffrey Hinton, introduced it in 1986 as part of backpropagation research
Yoav Freund and Robert Schapire, developed it for boosting algorithms in 1995
Leo Breiman, proposed it in 1996 as a variance reduction technique
Jerome Friedman, introduced it alongside Gradient Boosting in 1999 and 2001
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly states Bagging was 'Proposed by Leo Breiman in 1996.' Options A and B attribute it to researchers known for other algorithms (deep learning and AdaBoost respectively). Option D names Friedman who proposed Gradient Boosting, not Bagging.

jsonData.question: The 'Wisdom of the Crowds' principle in ensemble methods means that:
options: One strong model always outperforms a group of weak models on complex tasks
Each individual model in the ensemble is independently optimized for peak performance
Models are trained on the same data but use different optimization algorithms
Combining many weak learners creates a strong model better than any individual
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'Set of weak learners are combined to form a strong learner with better performance than any of them individually.' This is the 'wisdom of crowds' principle. Option A directly contradicts this — single strong models are not the ensemble goal.

jsonData.question: In Bagging regression, how is the final aggregated prediction computed from B trees?
options: Average of all B tree predictions: (1/B) × Σ f_b(x)
Maximum prediction across all B individual tree outputs
Weighted sum using learned performance-based weights for each tree
Median of all B tree predictions after sorting them in ascending order
jsonData.explanation: The formula shown in the lecture is f̂_agg(x) = (1/B) Σ_{b=1}^{B} f_b(x) — the arithmetic mean of all B trees. For classification, majority voting is used instead. Weighted sums describe Weighted Voting/Averaging, not standard Bagging.

jsonData.question: In Bagging, what are 'Out-of-Bag' (OOB) samples?
options: Bootstrap samples that are reused across multiple tree training iterations
Training examples NOT selected into a given bootstrap sample for that tree
Test set examples that are held completely out from all bootstrap samples
Samples removed because they are identified as outliers in the training data
jsonData.explanation: Each bootstrap sample uses only a subset of the data (with replacement). The unused samples — those not selected into a given bootstrap — are called Out-of-Bag (OOB) samples. The OOB error rate is computed over these unused samples across all bootstraps.

jsonData.question: What is the key difference between Random Forests and standard Bagging?
options: Random Forests use sequential training while Bagging uses parallel training
Random Forests aggregate using majority vote while Bagging uses averaging only
Random Forests limit each tree to k < m randomly selected features per split
Random Forests train each tree on a different random sample of training examples, just as in Bagging, but also add sequential error correction
jsonData.explanation: The key distinction: in Random Forests, each tree only considers k < m randomly selected features (without replacement) at each split. If k = m, it becomes standard Bagging. This feature restriction decorrelates the trees, reducing variance without increasing bias.

jsonData.question: According to the ensemble learning types diagram, what are the main goals of Bagging and Boosting respectively?
options: Bagging: reduce bias; Boosting: reduce variance
Bagging: exploit individual model strengths through performance-based weighting
Bagging: train models sequentially on errors; Boosting: train models in parallel
Bagging: reduce variance (overfitting); Boosting: reduce bias (underfitting)
jsonData.explanation: The diagram explicitly labels: Bagging's Main Goal = REDUCE VARIANCE (Overfitting), Boosting's Main Goal = REDUCE BIAS (Underfitting). Option A reverses these. Option B describes Weighted Voting/Averaging. Option C reverses the sequential/parallel property.

jsonData.question: Why are bagged trees described as identically distributed but correlated (i.d. not i.i.d.)?
options: All trees see the same features, so dominant features affect each tree equally
Each tree is trained on exactly the same bootstrap sample with the same features
Bootstrap sampling always produces the same data subset for each of the B trees
The aggregation step forces all trees to produce identical predictions on new data
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explains: 'they all have access to the same set of features. If a few features are very strong (dominant), they will influence every tree in the same way, causing the trees to be correlated.' This is why bagged trees are i.d. but NOT independent (not i.i.d.).

jsonData.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a strength (Pro) of Decision Trees in the lecture?
options: Can handle large datasets efficiently
Strong predictive performance that generalizes well to test data
Can handle mixed predictor types including continuous and qualitative features
Can easily handle missing data in the training examples
jsonData.explanation: The lecture lists 'Prediction performance is poor' and 'Does not generalize well' as CONS. Strong prediction performance is explicitly a weakness, not a strength. The pros include handling large datasets, mixed predictors, missing data, redundant variables, and easy interpretability when small.

jsonData.question: In the Boosting process, what happens immediately after evaluating the errors of the first weak model?
options: The first model's predictions are averaged with a second identically trained model
A new dataset is collected to directly address the misclassified examples
Higher weights are assigned to the misclassified examples for the next model
The final model is immediately produced by combining the first model with a stronger learner
jsonData.explanation: Boosting Step 3: 'Higher weights are assigned to the examples that were misclassified by the first model. This means the next model will focus more on correcting the errors.' Option D is wrong — the final model is only produced after many iterations, not immediately after the first.

jsonData.question: How does AdaBoost handle misclassified examples and individual model contributions?
options: It removes correctly classified samples to reduce dataset size each round
It computes the gradient of the loss function to determine the exact adjustments needed for each sample
It resamples the dataset with replacement using bootstrap to add more diversity
It increases weights of misclassified samples and adjusts each model's contribution weight
jsonData.explanation: AdaBoost 'focuses on correcting mistakes by increasing the weight of misclassified samples' and 'adjusts the weights for each model based on its performance.' This distinguishes it from Gradient Boosting (which uses gradients) and Bagging (which uses bootstrap sampling).

jsonData.question: What is the key difference between Gradient Boosting and AdaBoost?
options: Gradient Boosting minimizes error via gradient descent; AdaBoost reweights samples
AdaBoost uses gradient descent; Gradient Boosting uses sample reweighting instead
Both methods are identical in mechanism, differing only in their default tree depth
Gradient Boosting trains models in parallel; AdaBoost trains them sequentially here
jsonData.explanation: Gradient Boosting 'adds new models based on the gradient of the errors made by the previous model,' while AdaBoost corrects mistakes by 'increasing the weight of misclassified samples.' Option B reverses their mechanisms. Option D is wrong — both are sequential.

jsonData.question: How is XGBoost described relative to Gradient Boosting in the lecture?
options: A completely new algorithm with different mathematical foundations than Gradient Boosting
An optimized version of Gradient Boosting that is fast and efficient
A method that combines Gradient Boosting with random feature subsampling from RF
The original Gradient Boosting algorithm before any optimization was applied to it
jsonData.explanation: The lecture describes XGBoost as 'An optimized version of Gradient Boosting. Commonly used in competitions because it is fast and efficient.' It is not a new algorithm — it builds directly on Gradient Boosting with engineering optimizations (regularization, sparsity handling, etc.).

jsonData.question: In Random Forests, if the number of candidate features per split k equals the total features m, what does the algorithm become?
options: A standard single Decision Tree trained on the full feature set
A Boosting ensemble where trees are trained sequentially on residuals
Equivalent to standard Bagging where all features are available to every tree
A Stacking ensemble where each tree's output feeds a learned meta-model for prediction
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly states: 'If k = m, this is same as Bagging (all features are available).' Setting k = m removes the feature restriction that decorrelates trees, making Random Forest identical to Bagging. When k < m, it becomes Random Forests.

jsonData.question: When does Random Forest perform poorly according to the lecture?
options: When the training dataset is very large and the trees cannot be parallelized well
When k equals m, making Random Forest identical to standard Bagging in all aspects
When the number of bootstrap samples B is too small to achieve variance reduction
When there are many features but only a few are truly relevant to the target
jsonData.explanation: The lecture identifies: 'When you have large number of features, yet very small number of relevant features → Prob(selecting the relevant feature in k) is very small.' Random feature subsampling works poorly when only a tiny fraction of features are informative.

jsonData.question: In Stacking ensemble learning, what serves as the input features for training the meta-model?
options: The predictions made by the base models on the hold-out validation data
The raw original training features combined with the base models' parameters
The error residuals from each base model evaluated on the full training dataset
The final test set features passed directly through each of the trained base models
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'The base models' predictions serve as features for the meta-model.' The meta-model is trained on the hold-out validation data using the base models' predictions as its input. This is what distinguishes Stacking from simple ensembling.

jsonData.question: In Stacking, what are the two parts that the training dataset is split into, and what is each part used for?
options: Train set for meta-model training; a separate validation set to evaluate base model performance on
Training Data (for base models) and Hold-Out Validation Data (for training the meta-model)
Train set for weak learners; a separate test set used to evaluate the final stacked model
Bootstrap samples for each base model; a full retained dataset for meta-model retraining
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's table shows: Training Data → used to train base models; Hold-Out Validation Data → used to train the meta-model. The key insight is that the meta-model is trained on data the base models have NOT seen during their training, preventing overfitting.

jsonData.question: What is the fundamental structural difference between Boosting and Bagging in how models are trained?
options: Boosting uses different base model types while Bagging uses only decision trees
Bagging trains models sequentially focusing on errors; Boosting trains in parallel independently
Boosting trains models sequentially, each correcting the previous model's errors
Bagging samples without replacement while Boosting always samples with replacement here
jsonData.explanation: The ensemble types diagram states: Boosting 'Train models sequentially, each correcting the errors of the previous one,' while Bagging 'Train independent models in parallel on random subsets with replacement.' This sequential vs parallel distinction is the core structural difference.

jsonData.question: Compared to a single Decision Tree, what does Bagging sacrifice in exchange for better predictive performance?
options: Computational efficiency — Bagging requires substantially more memory than a single tree
Variance reduction — Bagging can paradoxically introduce more variance than one tree
Ability to handle missing data — Bagging cannot process incomplete feature vectors at all
Interpretability — Bagging loses the transparency that a single small decision tree offers
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly states Bagging 'loses on interpretability to single decision tree.' A single small tree is easy to visualize and understand (a listed pro). An ensemble of B trees is a black box. Options A, B, and C are not listed as tradeoffs of Bagging in the lecture.

jsonData.question: What is the default base_estimator (weak learner) used in sklearn's AdaBoostClassifier?
options: DecisionTreeClassifier
LogisticRegression
Support Vector Classifier (SVC with probability)
RandomForestClassifier
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'base_estimator: It uses DecisionTreeClassifier as default weak learner for training purpose. You can also specify different machine learning algorithms.' The base_estimator must accept sample weights — both Decision Trees and SVC qualify, but the DEFAULT is DecisionTreeClassifier.

jsonData.question: According to the ensemble types diagram, what is the main goal of Stacking (Stacked Generalization)?
options: Reduce variance by training independent models on different bootstrap data subsets
Maximize predictive power by combining diverse base models via a meta-model
Reduce bias by training models sequentially on previously misclassified examples
Exploit individual model strengths through validation-based weighted averaging
jsonData.explanation: The diagram labels Stacking's Main Goal as: 'MAXIMIZE PREDICTIVE POWER.' It does this by combining diverse base models (SVM, kNN, Neural Network, Decision Tree) in parallel; a meta-model learns how to best weight their predictions. Option C describes Boosting; option D describes Weighted Voting.

jsonData.question: In Weighted Voting/Averaging ensemble learning, how are the weights for each model determined?
options: Weights are fixed as equal for all models regardless of their individual performance
Weights are determined by the number of training examples each individual model was trained on
Weights are based on each individual model's accuracy on the validation set
Weights are randomly initialized and then refined by the meta-model during final training
jsonData.explanation: The diagram states: 'Weight based on individual model's accuracy on validation set.' Better-performing models receive higher weights. Option A describes simple (unweighted) voting. Option D describes a process from Stacking, not Weighted Voting.

jsonData.question: A researcher's single Decision Tree suffers from high variance. Which ensemble method most directly addresses this?
options: Boosting — because it reduces bias by focusing sequentially on hard misclassified examples
Stacking — because it combines diverse models to maximize overall predictive power
Weighted Voting — because it assigns higher weight to the best individual model's prediction
Bagging — because it directly targets variance reduction through parallel training on subsamples
jsonData.explanation: Bagging's main goal is explicitly 'REDUCE VARIANCE (Overfitting)' per the lecture. High variance in a single decision tree is exactly the problem Bagging solves by averaging B trees trained on bootstrap samples. Boosting targets bias (underfitting), not variance.

jsonData.question: Which of the following is explicitly listed as a CON of Decision Trees in the lecture?
options: Prediction performance is generally poor and the model does not generalize well
Cannot handle datasets with more than a few thousand training examples at once
Cannot process features that contain missing values in the training data
Requires all features to be standardized before splitting during tree construction
jsonData.explanation: The lecture lists three cons: (1) Prediction performance is poor, (2) Does not generalize well, (3) Large trees are hard to interpret. Options B and C contradict the listed PROS ('Can handle large datasets' and 'Can easily handle missing data'). Option D is not mentioned.

jsonData.question: According to the Bagging explanation, what happens when random errors from individual trees are averaged together?
options: Random errors accumulate and compound across trees, substantially increasing the final prediction variance
Random errors cancel each other out, resulting in a stable model with low variance
Random errors become the residuals that train the next tree in the boosting sequence
Random errors are identified and filtered out by the OOB evaluation before aggregation
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's Bagging flow states: 'The Result: The random errors cancel each other out, making the final model stable and accurate (Low Variance).' This error cancellation is the core mechanism by which averaging B trees reduces variance compared to a single tree.

jsonData.question: How can the Out-of-Bag (OOB) error rate be leveraged in Random Forests?
options: As a metric to measure correlation between the B trees during training iterations
As a validation metric for selecting the optimal number of candidate features k during construction
As a substitute for separate cross-validation to evaluate the Random Forest model directly
As a measure of each feature's contribution to the final model's prediction performance
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'The Out-of-Bag (OOB) error rate can be used to evaluate the Random Forest model, eliminating the need for separate cross-validation.' Each tree's OOB samples (unused in that tree's bootstrap) serve as a built-in held-out test set.

jsonData.question: Which correctly describes the final steps in the Stacking process?
options: Train meta-model on full test data → evaluate base models' errors on validation set
Train all base models simultaneously → combine predictions using simple majority voting
Generate base model predictions on the full training data and then apply simple majority voting as the final output prediction
Feed test data through base models into the meta-model → get final stacked predictions
jsonData.explanation: The final Stacking steps: base models predict on the test set → those predictions are input to the meta-model → the meta-model makes the final prediction. The meta-model was already trained on the hold-out validation predictions. Option B is simple voting, not Stacking.

jsonData.question: Why is training multiple trees on the identical dataset (without diversification) called a 'TRIVIAL solution' in the lecture?
options: All trees learn the same patterns, making the ensemble no better than a single tree
Each tree would independently overfit to different local regions of the training data
The aggregation of identical trees produces ambiguous or undefined predictions
Identical trees violate the mathematical convergence conditions for ensemble learning
jsonData.explanation: The lecture crosses out 'TRIVIAL Solution' with the note '(May be all trees learn the same thing).' If all trees see the same data, they all identify the same patterns, producing redundant predictions — the ensemble gains nothing over a single tree. Bootstrap Sampling solves this.

jsonData.question: In Bagging's bootstrap sampling, how are the B data subsets created from the training data?
options: Sampling without replacement, ensuring each example appears exactly once in each subset
Sampling with replacement from the training data to create B diverse overlapping subsets
Stratified sampling to maintain the original class distribution within each bootstrap subset
Random partitioning that divides training data into B equal non-overlapping subsets each time
jsonData.explanation: The lecture specifies: 'Collect B (≅ 100) subsets by sampling with replacement from training data.' Sampling WITH replacement means the same example can appear multiple times in one bootstrap sample, creating diverse subsets. Without-replacement partitioning is NOT bootstrap sampling.

jsonData.question: Recommendation Systems became an independent research field in the mid-1990s, originally starting with which technique?
options: Content-Based Filtering, which profiles items using metadata and keywords
Collaborative Filtering, based on shared user interaction patterns
Sequential RS, which models ordered chains of user actions over time
Graph-Learning RS, which uses network relationships between users and content
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'These systems became an independent research field in the mid-1990s, starting with Collaborative Filtering.' Content-Based, Sequential, and Graph-Learning RS are all later or distinct techniques — not the founding approach of the field.

jsonData.question: According to the lecture, Recommendation Systems suggest relevant items based on which two factors?
options: Item similarity and user profile characteristics
Search engine rankings and trending content metrics
Social media follower counts and demographic age groups
Published review scores and verified purchase timestamps
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly states: 'These suggestions are based on Item Similarity or User Profile Characteristics.' All other options describe external or unrelated criteria not part of the RS definition.

jsonData.question: The lecture states that a Recommendation System must interact flexibly with users to achieve two main goals. Which pair correctly identifies them?
options: Minimize server costs and maximize catalog coverage
Detect user locations and deliver geo-specific content
Understand user preferences and provide the most suitable suggestions
Build user activity logs and train classification models for item ranking
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly lists the two goals as: 'To understand the user's specific characteristics and preferences' and 'To provide the most suitable and accurate suggestions.' Options A, B, and D are not stated in the RS definition.

jsonData.question: The 'MusicBox' research case study described in the lecture combined which two signal types to generate music recommendations?
options: User demographic data and previously listened-to playlist metadata
Matrix factorization latent factors and cosine similarity from item tags
Purchase history data and product review sentiment from social platforms
Social tags (what people say about songs) and audio features (the sound itself)
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's MusicBox case study explicitly states it 'Combines Social Tags (what people say) with Audio Features (the sound itself)' to provide tailored music suggestions. The other options describe features of different systems (HPRS, general CF, e-commerce RS) — not MusicBox.

jsonData.question: What is the key difference between Content-Based Filtering and Collaborative Filtering according to the lecture?
options: Content-Based uses item features; CF uses historical user interactions
Content-Based requires far more training data; CF relies only on item keywords
Collaborative Filtering profiles items by metadata; Content-Based tracks digital footprints
Content-Based requires a user-item matrix; Collaborative Filtering applies TF-IDF to descriptions
jsonData.explanation: The lecture defines Content-Based as recommending based on item features (metadata, TF-IDF), while CF 'does not analyze the item's features. Instead, it relies on historical interactions.' Options C and D reverse the characteristic data source of each method.

jsonData.question: In Content-Based Recommendation Systems, what is the primary role of TF-IDF?
options: To decompose the user-item rating matrix into latent user and item factor matrices
To evaluate how important a word or item is within a document collection
To calculate the angle between item vectors and measure how close two items are
To predict missing rating values by minimizing a regularized mean squared error loss
jsonData.explanation: The lecture defines TF-IDF as 'a statistical measure used to evaluate how important a word is to a document in a collection. It helps the system understand the features of an item.' Matrix decomposition describes Matrix Factorization; angle-based similarity describes Cosine Similarity — these are separate tools.

jsonData.question: The Cosine Similarity formula Similarity(A,B) = A·B / (‖A‖‖B‖) is applied in Content-Based RS. What do vectors A and B represent?
options: The training loss value and the regularization coefficient of the current model
The total number of users and items currently stored in the recommendation matrix
The User Profile Vector and the Item Vector whose similarity is being measured
The TF-IDF document score and the inverse document frequency across the full corpus
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'we need to calculate the distance or similarity between the User Profile Vector (A) and the Item Vector (B).' The formula measures how 'close' or 'similar' these two vectors are after items are converted into vectors using TF-IDF.

jsonData.question: A user assigns a 4-star rating to a movie on Netflix. According to the lecture, this is classified as:
options: An implicit feedback signal, collected passively during the user's browsing session
Indirect data, because the system observed the user's behavior without explicit request
A digital footprint, since it leaves an interaction trace stored in the platform's logs
Explicit feedback, because the user intentionally and directly provided the rating
jsonData.explanation: Star ratings (1–5) are listed under explicit (direct) feedback. The lecture's key distinction: 'Direct (Explicit) Data — User Actively Provides Information, with explicit user intent.' Implicit (indirect) feedback includes browsing history, dwell time, and purchases — not intentional star ratings.

jsonData.question: Compared to explicit feedback, which set of properties best characterizes implicit feedback in Recommender Systems?
options: Abundant, automatically collected, but noisy since interactions may not reflect true preference
Scarce in volume, collected through active user input, accurate because it reflects stated preferences
Abundant in volume, collected actively when users submit ratings, highly accurate due to stated intent
Scarce in volume, collected passively from browsing events, accurate because it reflects engagement
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's comparison table shows implicit feedback is: 'Abundant, as it is collected automatically' (high volume), 'Easy to collect since gathered passively,' but 'Noisy, as interactions do not always indicate true preference.' Option B describes explicit feedback properties instead.

jsonData.question: What is the core reasoning principle behind Collaborative Filtering, as stated in the lecture?
options: Items are recommended by matching their metadata keywords against the user's stated preferences
Users who have liked the same items in the past are likely to share similar future preferences
The system builds a user profile from age, location, and demographics to make predictions
Recommendations are generated by analyzing item features using TF-IDF and cosine similarity
jsonData.explanation: The lecture describes CF's 'Logic of Similarity': 'If User A and User B both purchased and liked Item 1, the system marks them as Similar Users.' This shared-history assumption drives all CF recommendations. Options A and D describe Content-Based Filtering, not CF.

jsonData.question: A developer must build a CF system for a platform with 500 million users. Which CF variant does the lecture recommend, and why?
options: Memory-Based, because KNN scales linearly and handles billions of user interactions
Memory-Based, since no model training is needed, reducing all infrastructure requirements
Model-Based, because it scales to large data much better and achieves higher accuracy
Model-Based, because it relies directly on raw memory data without any training overhead
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's comparison table states Model-Based is 'Best For: Large, complex datasets' and is 'Highly accurate and handles large-scale data much better than memory-based methods.' Memory-Based 'can be slow with massive amounts of data.' Option D incorrectly describes Model-Based as using raw data without training — that is Memory-Based.

jsonData.question: A brand-new product is added to Amazon's catalog, but no user has ever purchased or rated it yet. Which CF challenge does this represent?
options: Data Sparsity, since most users interact with only about 1% of available catalog items
Scalability, as adding each new product degrades system performance across all user queries
Memory-Based failure, because KNN distance metrics cannot process entries with no ratings
Cold Start — New Item variant — since no interaction history exists for this product yet
jsonData.explanation: The lecture describes the Cold Start Problem — New Item scenario: 'A new product is added to the store. Since no one has rated it, the system cannot recommend it to anyone.' Data Sparsity refers to the low overall interaction rate (1%) across the entire catalog — not a single item's absence of ratings.

jsonData.question: What is the key distinction between Sequential and Session-based Recommendation Systems?
options: Sequential uses long-term history from known users; Session-based handles anonymous current activity
Sequential works with anonymous users and current mood; Session-based needs users' full history
Sequential predicts what a user needs right now; Session-based predicts the next item in a chain
Sequential exclusively uses Markov Chains; Session-based exclusively uses deep learning
jsonData.explanation: The SRS vs Session-based comparison table states: Sequential requires 'Long-term, time-stamped history' with a 'Known (Logged-in) User ID,' while Session-based uses only 'Recent, short-term interactions' and allows 'Anonymous (Guest/Unknown)' users. Options B and C swap the properties of the two systems.

jsonData.question: A guest user (not logged in) visits a travel site and searches for hotels in Paris. The site immediately recommends local Parisian restaurants. Which RS type does this best describe?
options: Sequential RS, because it tracks the user's full travel history to predict the logical next step
Session-based RS, because it acts on current in-session activity without needing a user identity
Content-Based Filtering, because it matches hotel and restaurant item metadata for similarity
Collaborative Filtering, because it finds other users who previously searched for Paris hotels
jsonData.explanation: Session-based RS 'focuses exclusively on the user's interactions during the Current Session' and is used when 'Users are often not logged in (Guests).' The lecture's own example mirrors this exactly: searching for hotels in a city triggers location-specific recommendations for that session only.

jsonData.question: In a user-item rating matrix, how does the lecture differentiate entries for explicit feedback versus implicit feedback?
options: Explicit entries are Boolean (0/1) watched indicators; implicit entries contain numerical star ratings
Both store numerical scores, but explicit ranges 1–10 while implicit ranges from 0 to 1
Explicit entries are star ratings (1–5, with '?' for unrated items); implicit entries are Boolean (0 or 1)
Explicit entries store ranked item lists; implicit entries store cosine similarity scores per item
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'For explicit feedback: each entry in r_ui is a numerical value, such as the number of stars... If the user did not rate the item, the value remains unknown (?).' 'For implicit feedback: the values are true/false (Boolean), indicating whether the user interacted with the item.' Option A reverses the two types.

jsonData.question: A new film is added to Netflix with no viewer ratings yet. Why does Content-Based Filtering handle this Cold Start scenario better than Collaborative Filtering?
options: Content-Based uses Matrix Factorization to fill in predicted ratings for new films automatically
Content-Based identifies early viewers of the film and uses their reactions to inform others
Content-Based requires fewer computing resources and is optimized for sparse rating matrices
Content-Based relies on item metadata like genre and keywords, so no prior viewing history is needed
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states Content-Based 'Works well in Cold-Start scenarios' because 'content-based systems rely on metadata instead of past interactions, they can still make recommendations.' CF requires interaction history, making it unable to handle a new item with zero ratings. Options A and B describe CF behaviors, not Content-Based.

jsonData.question: Which of the following is a limitation of Content-Based Filtering that the lecture explicitly identifies?
options: It treats each user in isolation and ignores preferences of other users with similar tastes
It relies on all historical user interaction data such as purchase records and past click behavior
It performs poorly on items that have very accurate and complete metadata in the catalog
It cannot scale to platforms with more than a few thousand users due to computational overhead
jsonData.explanation: The lecture's Disadvantages section states: 'Each user is treated separately — the system makes recommendations only based on what a single user has interacted with. It does not consider what other users like.' This is a core reason why CF, which leverages shared patterns across many users, often outperforms it.

jsonData.question: In Matrix Factorization, the predicted rating is $\hat{r}_{ui} \approx p_u \cdot q_i$. What do $p_u$ and $q_i$ represent?
options: The TF-IDF score of item i and the cosine similarity between user u and the item vector
User u's latent preference vector and item i's latent characteristic vector
The average rating user u gives across all items and item i's average across all users
The regularization penalty and the learning rate used in SGD to optimize the factorization
jsonData.explanation: The lecture defines: 'p_u is the vector representing user u's preferences' and 'q_i is the vector representing item i's characteristics.' These are learned latent vectors in a lower-dimensional space. Option D describes optimization hyperparameters, not the latent factor vectors that are being learned.

jsonData.question: In Probabilistic Matrix Factorization (PMF), the loss includes the term $\lambda(\|p_u\|^2 + \|q_i\|^2)$. What is the role of $\lambda$?
options: It sets the learning rate that controls how quickly SGD updates the latent factor vectors
It is the regularization coefficient that prevents latent vectors from overfitting to training ratings
It represents the number of latent dimensions used to decompose the rating matrix R
It is the overall mean rating used to normalize observed rating values before training begins
jsonData.explanation: The PMF loss is: L = Σ[(r_ui − r̂_ui)² + λ(‖p_u‖² + ‖q_i‖²)]. The λ term penalizes large latent vector norms, acting as L2 regularization to prevent overfitting. Option D describes μ from the SVD extension, not λ. Learning rate is a separate SGD hyperparameter.

jsonData.question: What key addition does the SVD model make to the basic PMF model, and what justifies this addition?
options: SVD adds an attention mechanism to capture sequential viewing patterns over long time horizons
SVD adds a confidence matrix c_ui because implicit interaction signals vary in their reliability
SVD adds dropout regularization layers to prevent overfitting in deep neural network components
SVD adds bias terms b_u and b_i to capture users' and items' systematic rating tendencies
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states SVD 'introduced bias terms in the model. b_u and b_i measure observed rating deviations of user u and item i.' Justification: 'some items widely receive better/worse ratings, and some users are consistently more/less generous.' Option B describes the implicit feedback confidence matrix, which is a separate extension.

jsonData.question: What does decomposing rating matrix R into user matrix P and item matrix Q accomplish in Collaborative Filtering?
options: It converts implicit Boolean feedback into explicit numerical star ratings on a 1–5 scale
It clusters all users into demographic groups for targeted recommendation campaigns
It predicts missing ratings so the system can recommend items users have not yet seen
It compresses item metadata into TF-IDF feature vectors for cosine similarity computation
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states: 'These matrices can then be used to reconstruct the original rating matrix and predict missing values, allowing us to recommend new items to users.' Matrix Factorization fills in the '?' entries in the sparse user-item matrix, enabling recommendations for items a user has never rated.

jsonData.question: In Matrix Factorization for Implicit Feedback, confidence is $c_{ui} = 1 + \alpha t_{ui}$. What does $t_{ui} = 0$ indicate, and what is the resulting $c_{ui}$?
options: The user interacted with the item heavily (e.g., watched it twice); c_ui equals 1 + 2α
The user never interacted with the item at all; c_ui equals exactly 1 (the minimum baseline)
The user watched 100% of the item once, so c_ui reaches its maximum possible value
The interaction status is unknown, so c_ui defaults to the global average confidence value
jsonData.explanation: When t_ui = 0, the formula gives c_ui = 1 + α·0 = 1. The lecture states t_ui = 0 means 'the user never watched i.' Simultaneously r_ui = 0 (no interaction recorded). A c_ui of 1 is the minimum baseline confidence used when there is no evidence of interest.

jsonData.question: A large music streaming service has millions of songs, but 95% of users never rate or review anything. Which approach does the lecture most strongly support for this scenario?
options: Content-Based Filtering only, since it avoids Cold Start and works entirely from song metadata
Memory-Based CF, since KNN-based distance metrics tend to scale best with millions of items
Model-Based CF with implicit feedback, handling large scale and behavioral signals efficiently
Session-based RS exclusively, since most users are anonymous and preferences shift per session
jsonData.explanation: The lecture states Model-Based CF is 'best for large, complex datasets' and is 'highly accurate.' The implicit feedback MF extension (c_ui = 1 + α·t_ui) handles behavioral signals like play counts without requiring explicit ratings. Memory-Based 'can be slow with massive amounts of data,' and Content-Based alone misses cross-user collaborative patterns.

jsonData.question: According to the lecture's taxonomy of Recommendation Systems (Figure 1), Collaborative Filtering is divided into which two sub-classes?
options: Content-Based and Knowledge-Based sub-approaches
Sequential and Session-based recommendation variants
Cross-domain and Context-aware recommendation methods
Memory-Based and Model-Based algorithm classes
jsonData.explanation: Figure 1 in the lecture shows Collaborative Filtering branching into 'Memory-Based' and 'Model-Based' methods. Sequential, Session-based, Hybrid, and Miscellaneous are separate top-level branches of the RS taxonomy — not sub-classes of Collaborative Filtering.

jsonData.question: In Memory-Based Collaborative Filtering, what is the difference between User-Based and Item-Based approaches?
options: User-Based finds similar users; Item-Based finds items similar to ones the user liked
User-Based analyzes item genre and keyword metadata; Item-Based profiles user demographics
User-Based uses Matrix Factorization; Item-Based relies on cosine similarity of TF-IDF features
User-Based is faster and scales better to large datasets; Item-Based requires more memory
jsonData.explanation: The lecture explicitly defines: 'User-Based: Recommends items by finding users with similar tastes' and 'Item-Based: Recommends items that are similar to items the user liked before.' Options B and C describe Content-Based methods. Option D misrepresents scalability — both memory-based variants struggle on large data, which is why model-based CF is preferred.

jsonData.question: Accuracy in classification is defined as:
options: The fraction of all examples that are classified correctly
The fraction of positive predictions that are actually positive
The fraction of all actual positive examples that were found
The harmonic mean of precision and recall for the positive class
jsonData.explanation: Accuracy = (correctly classified examples) / (total examples). It is the simplest performance measure but fails on imbalanced datasets. Option B defines Precision; Option C defines Recall; Option D defines F1.

jsonData.question: Precision (P) is formally defined as:
options: The fraction of actual positives that the model correctly predicted as positive
The fraction of positive predictions that are truly positive: TP / (TP + FP)
The fraction of negative examples that the model correctly predicted as negative
The harmonic mean of true positives and false positives in the confusion matrix
